notes.anchor: On the fifth day before the kalends of May
notes.note: Ante diem quintum Kalendas Maias = 27 April 43 BC; this was the date of the Senate session described, while the letter itself was written on 5 May ("third day before the nones of May").
Publius Servilius Vatia Isauricus the Younger (consul 48 BC with Julius Caesar), an influential senior senator who spoke in the Senate debate on pursuing Dolabella.
Publius Ventidius Bassus, a capable general and partisan of Mark Antony, declared a public enemy by the Senate in 43 BC; the motion of Servilius proposed that Brutus pursue him as well as Dolabella.

Cicero wants his son Marcus (serving under Brutus in Macedonia) elected to the college of augurs; augurs were the priestly board that interpreted omens, and membership was for life, making the position a mark of high social distinction.

notes.anchor: Gaius Marius, while he was in Cappadocia, was made an augur under the Lex Domitia
notes.note: The Lex Domitia de sacerdotiis (103 BC), proposed by Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus, transferred election of priests from co-optation by the colleges to popular vote in the tribal assembly; Marius's election while absent in Asia Minor established that physical absence was no bar.

notes.anchor: As long as there is only one patrician magistrate, the auspices cannot return to the patres
notes.note: Roman constitutional doctrine held that the auspices (divine authority to conduct public business) "reverted to the fathers" (patres) — the patrician senators — when no curule magistrates were in office; with only one consul remaining after Pansa's death, an interregnum could not legally be declared until both consular seats were vacant.

notes.anchor: The third day before the nones of May
notes.note: Ante diem tertium Nonas Maias = 5 May 43 BC, the date on which this letter was dispatched.
